1:
"And Paul, earnestly beholding the council, said, Men and brethren, I have lived in all good conscience before God until this day." The witnesses note Paul's bold gaze and his claim of a good conscience. On "earnestly beholding," the grammatical voices note his composed, fearless look. Gill: "looking wistly and intently at them... a modest cheerfulness, and a becoming boldness... as being not conscious of any guilt, and well assured of the goodness of his cause."

On "all good conscience," the witnesses note Paul's sincerity. He claims to have lived conscientiously before God — even, the witnesses note, in his former life as a persecutor, he had acted (though wrongly) in sincerity (1 Timothy 1:13). The claim asserts his integrity against the charges.

The puritan Henry notes Paul's confidence: brought before the Sanhedrin, he protests his own integrity, conscious of no guilt. The verse displays the apostle's bold, clear conscience before his judges.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, gazing steadily at the council (with composed boldness, conscious of no guilt, Gill), claimed to have lived in all good conscience before God — asserting his sincerity, even (the witnesses note) in his former zeal as a persecutor, when he acted wrongly but conscientiously (1 Timothy 1:13). The verse displays the apostle's bold, clear conscience before his judges. The verse marks the opening of Paul's defense before the Sanhedrin: his protest of integrity. The verse displays the confidence of a clear conscience: Paul, accused and in danger, looks his judges in the eye and claims to have lived sincerely before God. The verse displays the nature of conscience: Paul had always sought to serve God according to his light, even when his light was darkened by error. The claim of a good conscience, far from boasting, asserts his integrity against the false charges. The verse displays the boldness of the apostle: facing the hostile council, conscious of no guilt, he protests his sincere service to God. The clear conscience, the steady gaze, and the bold claim display the composure of the servant of Christ before his accusers. The verse opens the Sanhedrin scene with Paul's confident assertion of integrity, which would provoke the high priest's violent response (v.2).
2:
"And the high priest Ananias commanded them that stood by him to smite him on the mouth." The witnesses note the high priest's violent, illegal order. On Ananias, the grammatical voices note his identity and character. Robertson: "the son of Nebedaeus... Though high priest, he was a man of bad character." Barnes and Ellicott note his cruelty and injustice (later killed by the sicarii).

On the smiting, the witnesses note its illegality and provocation. Robertson: "The act was illegal and peculiarly offensive to a Jew at the hands of a Jew." The blow on the mouth was to silence Paul, in violation of all justice (as Jesus was struck, John 18:22).

The Reformed Calvin notes the high priest's rage unmasked: "the Lord did pluck this visure [mask] from his face, so that there was not found in him so much as the modesty of a mean man, but he poured out his furious force like a beast." The verse displays the council's disorder and Ananias's brutality.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Ananias the high priest (a man of bad character, cruel and unjust, Robertson, Barnes) commanded Paul struck on the mouth — an illegal, provocative act (offensive at a Jew's hands, Robertson), to silence him, as Jesus had been struck (John 18:22). Calvin notes the high priest's rage unmasked, pouring out fury like a beast, the Lord stripping off his pretended gravity. The verse displays the brutality and disorder of the council: the high priest, who should preside in justice, ordering an illegal blow. The verse marks the violent response to Paul's claim of integrity: Ananias, enraged, silencing him by force. The verse displays the injustice of the trial: the presiding officer commanding an assault on the accused before he could be heard. The verse displays the corruption of the Jerusalem leadership: the high priest, raging and lawless, embodying the disorder of a council estranged from God (Calvin). The illegal blow, paralleling the treatment of Jesus, displays Paul's fellowship in his Lord's sufferings. The verse marks the brutality that would provoke Paul's sharp rebuke (v.3): the high priest's lawless violence unmasking the injustice of the whole proceeding.
3:
"Then said Paul unto him, God shall smite thee, thou whited wall: for sittest thou to judge me after the law, and commandest me to be smitten contrary to the law?" The witnesses note Paul's sharp rebuke — and debate whether it was sinful. On "God shall smite thee," the witnesses largely read it as a prediction, not an imprecation. Barnes: "not to be regarded as an imprecation, or as an expression of angry feeling, but of a prediction... that a man so hypocritical and unjust could not escape the vengeance of God." The prophecy was fulfilled in Ananias's death.

On "whited wall," the witnesses note the proverbial term for a hypocrite (as Jesus's "whited sepulchres," Matthew 23:27, Barnes, Robertson). Paul exposes the high priest's hypocrisy: sitting to judge by the law, yet violating it.

On whether the rebuke was a fault, the witnesses divide. The Reformed Calvin defends it: "it is no curse... but rather a reprehension, joined with the denouncing of a punishment... a manifest and sharp accusation, if it proceed from a quiet mind, doth not pass the bounds set down by Christ." Wesley reads it as "a sudden and prophetic impulse." Robertson, however, notes: "More self-control might have served Paul better," seeing an indignant outburst that Paul confesses (v.5).

The witnesses agree: Paul rebuked the high priest — "God shall smite thee, thou whited wall" — for judging by the law yet violating it. They largely read "God shall smite thee" as a prediction, not an imprecation (Barnes), fulfilled in Ananias's death; and "whited wall" as the proverbial term for a hypocrite (Robertson). They divide on whether it was a fault: Calvin defends it as a lawful sharp rebuke from a quiet mind (not railing); Wesley as a prophetic impulse; but Robertson sees an indignant outburst that more self-control might have avoided, which Paul confesses (v.5). The verse displays Paul's exposure of the high priest's hypocrisy: judging by the law while breaking it. The verse marks the sharp rebuke, and the question of its rightness. The verse displays the tension between righteous indignation and Christian meekness: Paul's rebuke, defended by most as a just denunciation, yet seen by some as a lapse he acknowledged. The "whited wall" exposed the injustice of the proceeding: a judge violating the very law he claimed to uphold. The verse displays both the prophetic force of Paul's words (fulfilled in Ananias's death) and the debate over his manner — a just rebuke or an outburst confessed.
4:
"And they that stood by said, Revilest thou God's high priest?" The witnesses note the bystanders' rebuke of Paul. The grammatical voices note the charge of irreverence. Robertson: "Here was a charge of irreverence... The office called for respect." They reproach Paul for reviling the high priest, God's representative (Deuteronomy 17).

On the irony, Barnes notes: "It is remarkable that they who knew that he was not the high priest should have offered this language. He was, however, in the place of the high priest, and they might have pretended that respect was due to the office." The bystanders appeal to the office's dignity.

The Reformed Calvin notes the hypocrisy: "they look narrowly into other men's faults and wink at their own." They rebuke Paul for a sharp word, yet say nothing of Ananias's illegal violence. The verse displays the bystanders' partial indignation.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the bystanders rebuked Paul — "revilest thou God's high priest?" — charging him with irreverence toward the office (which called for respect, Robertson, Deuteronomy 17). Barnes notes the irony: they who knew Ananias was not properly the high priest still appealed to the office's dignity. Calvin notes the hypocrisy: they scrutinize Paul's sharp word yet wink at Ananias's illegal violence. The verse displays the bystanders' partial indignation: rebuking Paul, ignoring the high priest's lawlessness. The verse marks the charge of irreverence against Paul. The verse displays the hypocrisy of the council: indignant at a sharp word, silent at a brutal, illegal blow. The verse displays the appeal to the office's dignity: respect due to God's high priest, even when the man dishonors the office. The bystanders' rebuke, focusing on Paul's word while ignoring Ananias's violence, displays the partiality and hypocrisy Calvin condemns: scrutinizing others, excusing themselves. The verse sets up Paul's response (v.5): his acknowledgment of the law's command to honor the ruler, and the crux of whether he knew Ananias was the high priest. The bystanders' charge, appealing to the office, draws from Paul a striking reply that has occasioned much debate. The verse displays, in miniature, the moral inversion of the whole proceeding: a sharp word from the accused draws instant reproof, while the judge's lawless blow passes unremarked. The bystanders' selective zeal — quick to defend the office, blind to its abuse — is the very hypocrisy the gospel exposes, and the very disorder that would soon turn the council against itself.
5:
"Then said Paul, I wist not, brethren, that he was the high priest: for it is written, Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler of thy people." The witnesses note Paul's reply — the great crux of whether he knew, and the threefold interpretation. Ellicott summarizes: "(1) that St. Paul... literally did not know who it was that had given the order; (2) that the words were a somewhat ironical protest against the authority of Ananias as having been improperly appointed; (3) that the 'I wist not' stands for 'I did not consider.'"

Most of the grammatical voices favor literal ignorance. Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Ellicott: Paul, long absent, the office vacant, Ananias perhaps not in his robes or seat, the session irregular — Paul genuinely did not recognize him as high priest. Barnes notes this accords with the historical fact of the vacancy.

The Reformed Calvin (with Augustine) and Lightfoot favor irony: "this is a taunting excuse... Brethren, I acknowledge nothing in this man which belongeth to the priest." Paul, knowing the office, denied that Ananias deserved its honor. On the quotation (Exodus 22:28), all agree Paul affirms the duty to honor the ruler, recognizing the divine order in civil authority.

The witnesses agree: Paul replied "I wist not that he was the high priest," citing Exodus 22:28 (not to speak evil of the ruler). They divide on the meaning: most (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Ellicott) take it as literal ignorance — Paul, long absent, the office vacant, Ananias not in robes or seat, genuinely not recognizing him; Calvin (with Augustine) and Lightfoot take it as irony — Paul denying that Ananias deserved the honor of the office; some (Grotius, Doddridge) as "I did not consider," an apology. All agree the quotation affirms the duty to honor the ruler, recognizing God's order in civil authority (Romans 13). The verse displays Paul's reply and his reverence for the office. The verse marks the crux: did Paul not know, speak ironically, or apologize? The verse displays the apostle's respect for authority: whatever the sense, he affirms the law's command to honor the ruler. The threefold debate — literal ignorance, irony, or apology — turns on the historical situation and Paul's tone. The verse displays the principle that civil and appointed authority is ordained by God and to be honored, even when its holders dishonor it. Paul's reply, defending himself yet affirming the duty to respect the ruler, displays both his integrity and his submission to the divine order in authority.
6:
"But when Paul perceived that the one part were Sadducees, and the other Pharisees, he cried out in the council, Men and brethren, I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee: of the hope and resurrection of the dead I am called in question." The witnesses note Paul's stratagem of dividing the council and his confession of the resurrection. On the stratagem, Chrysostom defends it: "both in this, and in what comes after it, he wished to divide the multitude, which had an evil unanimity against him. And he does not speak a falsehood here either: for he was a Pharisee by descent."

On the perceiving and crying out, the grammatical voices note Paul's prudence. Clarke: "finding he was not to expect any justice, [he] thought it best thus to divide the council." Ellicott notes it prevented the hasty unanimous condemnation that had doomed Stephen.

On "the hope and resurrection," the witnesses note this was the heart of the matter. The resurrection — of Christ and of the dead — was the true point of Paul's gospel, the issue between him and his accusers. The puritan Henry notes Paul's prudence and ingenuity standing him in stead, the Lord delivering him.

The witnesses agree: Paul, perceiving the council was part Pharisee and part Sadducee, cried out that he was a Pharisee, called in question for the hope and resurrection of the dead — a stratagem to divide the council (Chrysostom: not a falsehood, he was a Pharisee by descent), preventing a hasty unanimous condemnation (Ellicott, as had doomed Stephen). The resurrection was the true heart of the matter, the issue between Paul and his accusers (Henry: his prudence standing him in stead, the Lord delivering him). The verse displays Paul's prudent stratagem and his confession of the resurrection. The verse marks the turning of the trial: Paul dividing his enemies by appealing to the doctrine that split them. The verse displays both the apostle's shrewdness (using the division to his advantage) and the centrality of the resurrection to his gospel. The stratagem, defended by Chrysostom as truthful (Paul genuinely a Pharisee, genuinely contending for the resurrection), displays his prudence in a hostile court. The verse displays the resurrection as the cardinal point: not a mere tactic, but the real issue — for the hope of the resurrection, grounded in Christ's rising, was the heart of Paul's preaching. The verse displays God's providence using Paul's wisdom to deliver him from the united hostility of the council.
7:
"And when he had so said, there arose a dissension between the Pharisees and the Sadducees: and the multitude was divided." The brief verse notes the division of the council. The grammatical voices note the immediate effect. Barnes: "The Pharisees embraced, as he desired and expected, his side of the question, and became his advocates." Paul's words split the council along its doctrinal fault-line.

On the strategic success, Ellicott notes: "As a strategic act St. Paul's words had immediately the effect which he desired. They prevented the hasty unanimous vote." The division saved Paul from united condemnation.

The witnesses note the dissension (stasis) was strife, not mere disagreement (Robertson). The verse displays the council turning on itself, the Pharisees defending Paul against the Sadducees.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's words split the council — the Pharisees taking his side against the Sadducees (Barnes) — preventing the hasty unanimous condemnation (Ellicott); the dissension was strife, not mere disagreement (Robertson). The verse displays the division of the council: the Pharisees and Sadducees at strife over the resurrection. The verse marks the success of Paul's stratagem: the council turning on itself, his enemies divided. The verse displays the providence in the division: the united hostility broken, Paul defended by one party against the other. The verse displays the deep doctrinal rift in Judaism: the resurrection splitting Pharisee from Sadducee, a fault-line Paul exploited. The division, far from a mere tactic, exposed the real issue — the resurrection — and broke the council's evil unanimity. The verse displays God's providence using the doctrinal division to deliver Paul: the council, gathered to condemn him, now at war with itself. The strife between Pharisees and Sadducees, sparked by Paul's confession of the resurrection, marks the unraveling of the trial and the providential preservation of the apostle. The verse sets up the Pharisees' defense of Paul (v.9) and the captain's rescue (v.10): the divided council unable to condemn, Paul delivered through their strife. The verse displays the swiftness with which a body united in malice can fracture along its own deepest seams. The resurrection, which Paul confessed as the ground of his hope, was the one question on which his judges could never agree; and the strife it provoked, far from a mere accident of the moment, laid bare the divided heart of a Judaism that had rejected its risen Lord.
8:
"For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, neither angel, nor spirit: but the Pharisees confess both." The witnesses note the doctrinal difference between the sects. On the Sadducees' denials, the grammatical voices note their materialism. Barnes: "they understood that there was no future state, and that the soul did not exist after death... They were materialists." They denied the resurrection, angels, and spirits.

On "both" for three items, the witnesses note the grouping. Calvin and Robertson note "both" because angel and spirit are grouped together (the soul and angels being of like nature). The Pharisees confessed the resurrection and the existence of angels and spirits.

The Reformed Calvin notes the gravity of the Sadducees' error and the difference from the Epicureans: the Sadducees confessed God's providence but confined rewards to this life — yet their denial of the resurrection and the soul's immortality was a "detestable monster" to be fled. He affirms the immortality of human and angelic spirits.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Sadducees denied the resurrection, angels, and spirits — materialists holding the soul died with the body (Barnes) — while the Pharisees confessed both (the resurrection, and angels/spirits, grouped as "both" because of their like nature, Calvin, Robertson). Calvin notes the gravity of the Sadducees' error, a "detestable monster" to be fled, affirming the immortality of human and angelic spirits. The verse displays the doctrinal fault-line: the Sadducees' materialism against the Pharisees' belief in resurrection and spirits. The verse marks the explanation of the division (v.7): the deep difference over the resurrection and the spiritual world. The verse displays the error of the Sadducees: denying the resurrection, the soul's immortality, and the existence of angels and spirits. The verse displays the truth the Pharisees held (and Paul affirmed): the resurrection of the dead and the reality of the spiritual world. The doctrinal note — the Sadducees' denials, the Pharisees' confession — explains the strife and grounds Paul's appeal. Calvin's treatment affirms against all materialism the immortality of the soul and the reality of angels and spirits. The verse displays the cardinal doctrines at stake: the resurrection, the soul's survival, the spiritual world — denied by the Sadducees, confessed by the Pharisees, and held by Paul as the heart of the gospel hope.
9:
"And there arose a great cry: and the scribes that were of the Pharisees' part arose, and strove, saying, We find no evil in this man: but if a spirit or an angel hath spoken to him, let us not fight against God." The witnesses note the Pharisees' defense of Paul. On "we find no evil," the grammatical voices note the verdict of innocence. Barnes notes the parallel with Pilate's verdict on Jesus (John 19:6). The Pharisees, against the Sadducees, declare Paul innocent.

On "if a spirit or an angel hath spoken to him," the witnesses note they referred to Paul's vision (22:17-18). Barnes notes they perverted his statement (Paul said the Lord spoke to him, not a spirit or angel), using it to oppose the Sadducees. Their motive was hatred of the Sadducees, not love of Paul.

On "let us not fight against God," the witnesses note the echo of Gamaliel (5:39) — though the clause is textually doubtful (Clarke, Ellicott). Ellicott: "The line taken by the Pharisees is altogether that of Gamaliel... they have not got further than the cautious policy of those who halt between two opinions."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Pharisees defended Paul — "we find no evil in this man" (a verdict of innocence, like Pilate's on Jesus, Barnes) — suggesting a spirit or angel may have spoken to him (referring to his vision, 22:17-18, but perverting it, Barnes, since Paul said the Lord spoke), their motive being hatred of the Sadducees, not love of Paul. The "let us not fight against God" echoes Gamaliel (5:39), though textually doubtful (Clarke, Ellicott); the Pharisees still halt between two opinions (Ellicott). The verse displays the Pharisees' defense: declaring Paul innocent to spite the Sadducees. The verse marks the council's reversal: those who would have condemned Paul now defend him. The verse displays the irony: the Pharisees, defending Paul from party-spirit, unwittingly bear witness to his innocence. The verse displays the cautious, half-believing stance of the Pharisees: granting Paul may have received a revelation, yet not committing to the gospel. The defense, sprung from hatred of the Sadducees, providentially served Paul's deliverance. The verse displays God's providence using the council's division: the Pharisees' party-spirit issuing in a verdict of innocence, the very men who might have condemned Paul now protecting him. The Pharisees' defense, however self-interested, marks the breaking of the united hostility and the preservation of the apostle.
10:
"And when there arose a great dissension, the chief captain, fearing lest Paul should have been pulled in pieces of them, commanded the soldiers to go down, and to take him by force from among them, and to bring him into the castle." The witnesses note the captain's second rescue of Paul. On the dissension, the grammatical voices note the violence. Robertson: the soldiers were to "seize and save Paul from the midst of the rabbis or preachers (in their rage to get at each other)." The strife threatened to tear Paul apart.

On the captain's fear, the witnesses note his responsibility for a Roman citizen. Ellicott: "The fear of the chiliarch was naturally heightened by his knowledge that he was responsible for the life of a Roman citizen." He rescued Paul to the castle.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence: "as he was appointed to be the minister of God's providence to save Paul's life, so he delivereth him now the second time by his soldiers from death... the Lord, who from heaven provided help for his servant, doth direct his blind hands thither." The verse displays the second deliverance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as the strife grew violent (threatening to tear Paul apart, Robertson), the chief captain, fearing for the Roman citizen (Ellicott), commanded the soldiers to seize Paul and bring him to the castle — the second rescue. Calvin notes the providence: the captain, minister of God's providence, delivering Paul again, the Lord directing his blind hands. The verse displays the second deliverance: Paul rescued from the violent council by the Roman soldiers. The verse marks the captain's intervention: snatching Paul from the strife to the safety of the castle. The verse displays God's providence preserving Paul: the council's division issuing in violence, the Roman power again delivering him. The verse displays the role of the civil authority: the captain, responsible for a Roman citizen, rescuing Paul from the murderous strife. Calvin's observation stands: the Lord directs even the captain's actions for his servant's preservation. The verse displays the second rescue (after the riot, 21:31): God repeatedly delivering Paul through the Roman power. The captain's intervention, halting the violence and securing Paul in the castle, marks the providence that preserved the apostle for his witness at Rome (v.11). The verse displays the divine care working through the Roman authority to save Paul from the fury of the divided council.
11:
"And the night following the Lord stood by him, and said, Be of good cheer, Paul: for as thou hast testified of me in Jerusalem, so must thou bear witness also at Rome." The witnesses note the Lord's encouragement and the promise of Rome. On "the Lord stood by him," the grammatical voices note it was Jesus. Robertson: "Paul never needed Jesus more than now... In every crisis Jesus appears to him." The Lord came to comfort his discouraged servant.

On "be of good cheer," the witnesses note Paul's need. Ellicott: the anxious questions of the wakeful night — was his work to be cut short? would he reach Rome? — answered by the Lord's "be of good cheer." The vision approved Paul's conduct and assured him of the future.

On "so must thou bear witness also at Rome," the witnesses note the divine commission and providence. Barnes: this fulfilled Paul's desire (Romans 1:10) and made his journey to Rome certain (28:30-31). The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort was not deliverance but the promise that his trials would issue in witness at Rome — teaching us to rest on God's word even when help is delayed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Lord Jesus stood by Paul (appearing in his crisis, Robertson) and said "be of good cheer" — answering his anxious questions (would his work be cut short? would he reach Rome? Ellicott) — and promised "so must thou bear witness also at Rome," the divine commission making the journey certain (Barnes, fulfilled 28:30-31). Calvin notes the comfort was not immediate deliverance but the assurance that his trials would issue in witness at Rome. The verse displays the Lord's gracious encouragement: comforting his discouraged servant and confirming his future. The verse marks the divine commission to Rome: Paul's long-cherished hope (Romans 1:10) now made certain. The verse displays the providence and promise: the Lord approving Paul's witness at Jerusalem and ordaining his witness at Rome. The encouragement, coming at the darkest hour, displays Christ's care for his servant in crisis. The promise of Rome, far from immediate rescue, assured Paul that his sufferings had a purpose and an end. The verse displays the foundation of Paul's confidence through the trials ahead: the Lord's word that he must bear witness at Rome — therefore he would survive the plots, the storms, and the imprisonment. The divine commission, comforting and certain, sustains Paul through all that follows.
12:
"And when it was day, certain of the Jews banded together, and bound themselves under a curse, saying that they would neither eat nor drink till they had killed Paul." The witnesses note the murderous conspiracy. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the desperate plot: certain Jews bound themselves under a curse (an anathema) to kill Paul, vowing not to eat or drink until they had done so.

On the curse, the witnesses note its rashness. Chrysostom: "See how vehement and revengeful they are in their malice!... Behold fasting the mother of murder!" The vow bound them to murder, fasting in the service of bloodshed.

On the escape clause, Lightfoot notes the rabbis could absolve such vows: "He that hath made a vow not to eat any thing... let him go to the wise men, and they will loose his vow." So the conspirators risked little by the oath. The verse displays the depth of the malice against Paul.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: certain Jews bound themselves under a curse to kill Paul, vowing not to eat or drink until they had — a desperate, murderous conspiracy (Henry). Chrysostom marks the malice: fasting the mother of murder, the vow binding them to bloodshed. Lightfoot notes the rabbis could absolve such vows, so the conspirators risked little. The verse displays the depth of the hatred against Paul: more than forty men vowing his death. The verse marks the conspiracy: a plot to murder the apostle, sworn under a curse. The verse displays the desperate malice of Paul's enemies: unable to condemn him legally, they resort to assassination. The verse displays the perversion of religious vows: a curse and a fast sworn in the service of murder. The conspiracy, the most determined attempt yet on Paul's life, displays the relentless hatred that pursued him. The verse displays the danger Paul faced even after the Lord's promise (v.11): the plot that would have killed him but for God's providence (the nephew, the captain). The murderous vow, sworn by more than forty men, marks the extremity of the peril — and sets the stage for the providential deliverance that would carry Paul safely to Caesarea and toward Rome. The verse displays both the malice of his enemies and the certainty of the divine promise that he must yet bear witness at Rome.
13:
"And they were more than forty which had made this conspiracy." The brief verse notes the number of conspirators. The grammatical voices note the size of the band. Robertson and others note more than forty men joined the plot — a large, determined conspiracy. Chrysostom notes the perverse unanimity: "when it is for an evil object, the entire people does it."

The witnesses note the gravity of the threat. Forty determined assassins, sworn to kill Paul, posed a serious danger, requiring the large military escort that followed (v.23).

The verse marks the extent of the conspiracy: not a few but more than forty, a formidable threat to Paul's life. The number underscores the providence that delivered him.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: more than forty men joined the conspiracy — a large, determined band of assassins (Robertson), displaying the perverse unanimity in evil (Chrysostom: for an evil object, the whole people acts). The verse displays the size of the threat: forty sworn murderers. The verse marks the gravity of the conspiracy: a formidable band, requiring the large escort that followed (v.23). The verse displays the determination of Paul's enemies: not a few but more than forty, sworn to his death. The verse displays the providence that delivered Paul from so great a threat: the plot of forty assassins foiled by the nephew's warning and the captain's escort. The number — more than forty — underscores both the malice against Paul and the magnitude of his deliverance. The verse displays the perverse zeal of the conspirators: united in evil, sworn to murder, a danger that would have overwhelmed an ordinary man. Yet the divine promise (v.11) stood: Paul must bear witness at Rome, and no conspiracy could thwart it. The verse displays the seriousness of the plot and the certainty of God's protection: forty sworn assassins against the one man God had promised to preserve. The number marks the extremity of the peril and the greatness of the providence that carried Paul safely through. The verse displays the grim arithmetic of the conspiracy: more than forty men, each bound by the same dreadful oath, each staking his life on the death of one apostle. The number is recorded not for its own sake but to magnify the deliverance that followed — for against this sworn band stood only a young nephew's warning and the unseen hand of the God who had promised Paul should reach Rome.
14:
"And they came to the chief priests and elders, and said, We have bound ourselves under a great curse, that we will eat nothing until we have slain Paul." The witnesses note the conspirators enlisting the leaders. The grammatical voices note the involvement of the chief priests and elders. The assassins came to the leaders, disclosing their vow and seeking their cooperation in the plot (v.15).

Chrysostom marks the corruption of the leaders: "they ought to have come (to the tribune), ought to have laid a charge... but for captains of banditti, these are not the doings for rulers, but for ruffians." The leaders, instead of justice, joined a murder plot.

The Reformed Calvin and others note the depth of the corruption: the chief priests, who should uphold the law, conspiring in assassination. The verse displays the collusion of the leadership in the murderous plot.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conspirators came to the chief priests and elders, disclosing their vow to kill Paul and seeking their cooperation. Chrysostom marks the corruption: the leaders, who should have sought justice, joining a plot fit for banditti, not rulers. The verse displays the collusion of the leadership in the murder plot. The verse marks the involvement of the chief priests and elders: the religious authorities conspiring in assassination. The verse displays the depth of the corruption: the leaders of Israel, who should uphold the law, joining a murderous conspiracy against an innocent man. The verse displays the perversion of the priesthood: the chief priests, sworn to God's service, plotting murder. The collusion of the leaders, far from restraining the conspirators, abetted them — displaying the moral bankruptcy of the Jerusalem leadership. The verse displays the unholy alliance: the assassins and the priests united against Paul. The leaders' readiness to join the plot displays how far they had fallen from justice and godliness — the very men who should have protected Paul conspiring in his murder. The verse sets up the plot to lure Paul to his death (v.15): the leaders agreeing to request his appearance, that the assassins might kill him on the way. The collusion of the leadership marks the depth of the malice and the corruption that Paul faced at Jerusalem.
15:
"Now therefore ye with the council signify to the chief captain that he bring him down unto you to morrow, as though ye would enquire something more perfectly concerning him: and we, or ever he come near, are ready to kill him." The witnesses note the treacherous plan. The grammatical voices note the deception: the council was to ask the captain to bring Paul down, under pretext of further inquiry, so the assassins could kill him on the way.

On the treachery, the witnesses note the abuse of legal forms. The conspirators planned to use the council's authority as a cover for murder, the pretended inquiry masking the ambush.

The witnesses note the desperation and cunning of the plot. Having failed to condemn Paul legally, the conspirators devise an assassination cloaked in a legal request. The verse displays the treacherous scheme.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conspirators planned for the council to request Paul's appearance, under pretext of further inquiry, so they could kill him on the way — a treacherous scheme abusing legal forms to mask murder. The verse displays the cunning of the plot: the pretended inquiry cloaking the ambush. The verse marks the treacherous plan: the council's authority used as a cover for assassination. The verse displays the depth of the conspirators' malice and cunning: unable to condemn Paul, they devise a murder cloaked in legal pretext. The verse displays the corruption of the proceeding: the council asked to lend its authority to a sham inquiry masking an ambush. The treacherous plan, using the forms of justice to accomplish murder, displays the moral bankruptcy of Paul's enemies. The verse displays the desperate cunning of the plot: every legal avenue having failed, the conspirators resort to deception and assassination. The scheme, depending on the council's collusion, would have succeeded but for the providential warning (v.16). The verse displays the peril Paul faced: a carefully laid plot, backed by the leaders, to murder him under the cover of a legal request. The treacherous plan marks the extremity of the danger and sets up the providential deliverance through Paul's nephew, who would hear and expose the plot.
16:
"And when Paul's sister's son heard of their lying in wait, he went and entered into the castle, and told Paul." The witnesses note the providential discovery by Paul's nephew. The grammatical voices note this is the only mention of Paul's family. Paul's sister's son, somehow learning of the plot, came to the castle and warned Paul.

On the providence, the Reformed Calvin notes the divine care: "Again he is saved by man's forethought" (Chrysostom). Though the Lord had promised Paul's safety (v.11), he used the nephew's warning as the means.

The witnesses note the access to Paul. The nephew could enter the castle, suggesting Paul's confinement was not close, and that he had family connections. How the young man learned of the plot is not said. The verse displays the providential warning.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's sister's son (the only mention of his family) heard of the plot and came to the castle to warn Paul — a providential discovery (Chrysostom: again saved by man's forethought), the means God used to fulfill his promise of Paul's safety (v.11, Calvin). The nephew's access to the castle suggests Paul's confinement was not close. The verse displays the providential warning: the plot exposed by Paul's nephew. The verse marks the deliverance from the conspiracy: the young man's warning thwarting the assassins. The verse displays God's providence working through human means: the Lord's promise of safety (v.11) fulfilled through the nephew's timely warning. The verse displays the divine care for Paul: even the secret plot of forty men brought to light through a young relative. The verse displays the harmony of divine promise and human agency: God had promised Paul would reach Rome, and he used the nephew's forethought to preserve him. The only glimpse of Paul's family — his sister's son — appears at this crucial moment of deliverance. The verse displays the providence that watched over the apostle: the conspiracy, however carefully laid, exposed by an unexpected witness. The nephew's warning, the instrument of God's protection, marks the providential foiling of the plot and the preservation of Paul for his witness at Rome.
17:
"Then Paul called one of the centurions unto him, and said, Bring this young man unto the chief captain: for he hath a certain thing to tell him." The witnesses note Paul's prudent action. The grammatical voices note his use of means. Despite the Lord's promise (v.11), Paul did not presume but acted wisely, sending the young man to the captain.

Chrysostom marks the balance of faith and prudence: "even after He has appeared to him, He again suffers him to be saved by man's means... because he believed, he did not therefore sleep: no; what was in his own power by means of human wisdom, he did not abandon." Paul trusted the promise yet used means.

The Reformed Calvin notes the same: Paul's faith did not make him careless, but he diligently used the means of his preservation. The verse displays the apostle's prudent use of means.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul called a centurion and sent his nephew to the captain with the warning — acting prudently despite the Lord's promise (v.11). Chrysostom marks the balance: even after the vision, Paul was saved by human means, believing yet not sleeping, using the wisdom in his power. Calvin notes faith did not make Paul careless but diligent in using means. The verse displays the apostle's prudent action: trusting the promise yet employing the means of deliverance. The verse marks the wise use of means: Paul sending the young man to the captain. The verse displays the harmony of faith and prudence: Paul, assured of God's protection, yet acting wisely to secure it. The verse displays the model of trust: believing the promise does not mean neglecting the means; Paul trusted God and used human wisdom. Chrysostom's observation is instructive: the believer, even with a divine promise, does not become careless but employs all lawful means. The verse displays Paul's calm, prudent response to the plot: not presuming on the promise, but acting to preserve his life. The wise use of means, joined to faith in the promise, displays the balance of the mature believer: resting on God's word while diligently doing what is in his power. The verse marks Paul's prudent handling of the danger, sending the warning through the proper channel to the captain.
18:
"So he took him, and brought him to the chief captain, and said, Paul the prisoner called me unto him, and prayed me to bring this young man unto thee, who hath something to say unto thee." The brief verse notes the centurion conducting the nephew to the captain. The grammatical voices note the centurion's compliance: he took the young man and brought him to the captain, relaying Paul's request.

On "Paul the prisoner," the witnesses note the title. Paul, though a prisoner, was treated with respect, his request honored by the centurion. His Roman citizenship secured him courteous treatment.

The witnesses note the orderly process: the request relayed, the young man conducted, the captain about to hear the warning. The verse displays the providential chain of means.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the centurion took the young man to the captain, relaying Paul's request — Paul, though "the prisoner," treated with respect, his request honored (his Roman citizenship securing courteous treatment). The verse displays the orderly relay of the warning: the centurion conducting the nephew to the captain. The verse marks a link in the providential chain: the warning passing from Paul to the centurion to the captain. The verse displays the respect shown Paul even as a prisoner: his request promptly honored. The verse displays the means of deliverance unfolding step by step: the nephew brought, the captain about to hear the plot. The verse, brief as it is, advances the providential rescue: each link in the chain — the nephew's hearing, Paul's sending, the centurion's compliance — bringing the warning to the captain who could act. The verse displays the courteous treatment of Paul: "the prisoner" whose request the centurion honored without delay. The orderly process, the respect shown the apostle, and the relay of the warning display the providence working through ordinary means to preserve Paul. The verse marks the conducting of the nephew to the captain, the next step in the foiling of the plot. The providential chain — hearing, warning, relaying — advances toward the captain's decisive action (vv.23-24) that would carry Paul safely to Caesarea.
19:
"Then the chief captain took him by the hand, and went with him aside privately, and asked him, What is that thou hast to tell me?" The witnesses note the captain's kind, private attention. The grammatical voices note his courtesy. He took the young man by the hand and led him aside privately, asking what he had to tell — a gentle, considerate manner toward the youth.

On the privacy, the witnesses note the captain's prudence. Wishing to learn the matter without alerting others, he drew the young man aside, ensuring the plot could be addressed secretly.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence in the captain's care: God governing the captain's kindness to preserve Paul. The verse displays the captain's courteous, prudent inquiry.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the captain took the young man by the hand and led him aside privately, asking what he had to tell — a courteous, considerate manner, and a prudent privacy to learn the matter without alerting others. Calvin notes the providence in the captain's kindness, God governing it to preserve Paul. The verse displays the captain's gentle, prudent inquiry. The verse marks the captain's attentive care: taking the youth by the hand, drawing him aside, asking kindly. The verse displays the providence working through the captain's courtesy: his willingness to hear the warning, his prudence in privacy. The verse displays the captain's character: kind toward the young man, careful in handling the matter. The taking by the hand, the private aside, the gentle question display a considerate and capable officer. The verse displays God's providence in the captain's disposition: ready to listen, prudent to act secretly. The captain's courteous inquiry, drawing out the warning privately, marks the providential means of Paul's deliverance — the right officer, willing to hear, careful to act. The verse displays the kindness shown to Paul's nephew and the prudence that would address the plot without alarming the conspirators. The captain's gentle, private inquiry advances the rescue, drawing out the details of the plot (vv.20-21) that he would act to thwart. The verse displays how God's purposes ride upon the smallest courtesies of ordinary men. The captain's willingness to take a young stranger by the hand, to step aside, to listen — these unremarkable kindnesses became the hinge on which Paul's life turned. Providence does not always thunder; here it works through a Roman officer's patient, private attention to a boy with a secret.
20:
"And he said, The Jews have agreed to desire thee that thou wouldest bring down Paul to morrow into the council, as though they would enquire somewhat of him more perfectly." The witnesses note the nephew's report of the plot. The grammatical voices note the accurate disclosure: the young man relays the conspirators' plan (v.15) — to request Paul's appearance under pretext of further inquiry.

On the report, the witnesses note its precision. The nephew faithfully conveys the scheme, enabling the captain to understand and thwart it.

The witnesses note the providence in the disclosure: the secret plot laid bare through the young man's report. The verse displays the exposure of the conspiracy.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the nephew reported the plot — the Jews' plan to request Paul's appearance under pretext of inquiry (the scheme of v.15) — faithfully conveying the conspiracy to the captain. The verse displays the exposure of the plot: the secret scheme laid bare. The verse marks the disclosure: the young man relaying the conspirators' plan. The verse displays the providence in the report: the hidden plot brought to light, enabling the captain to act. The verse displays the faithful, precise disclosure: the nephew conveying the scheme exactly, that the captain might understand the danger. The verse displays God's providence working through the young man's report: the secret conspiracy of forty men exposed by a single witness. The accurate report, the captain's attentive hearing, and the impending action display the means of Paul's deliverance. The verse displays the unraveling of the plot: the conspirators' careful scheme, depending on secrecy, undone by the nephew's disclosure. The verse marks the key moment of exposure: the captain now knowing the plan, able to thwart it. The faithful report, conveying the treacherous request and the pretended inquiry, displays the providence that preserved Paul — the plot revealed in time to be foiled. The verse advances the rescue, the captain about to take decisive action (vv.23-24) to carry Paul safely beyond the reach of the conspirators. The verse displays the unraveling of a scheme that depended wholly on secrecy. The conspirators had counted on the council's request seeming a routine inquiry; but the moment their pretext was spoken aloud to the captain, the whole design lay open. What was meant to look like justice was now exposed as ambush, and the plot began to collapse under the weight of its own disclosure.
21:
"But do not thou yield unto them: for there lie in wait for him of them more than forty men, which have bound themselves with an oath, that they will neither eat nor drink till they have killed him: and now are they ready, looking for a promise from thee." The witnesses note the nephew's warning and plea. The grammatical voices note the detail: more than forty men in ambush, bound by oath, ready and awaiting only the captain's consent. The young man urges him not to yield.

On the readiness of the plot, the witnesses note its imminence. The conspirators were ready, waiting only for the captain's promise to bring Paul down. The warning came just in time.

The witnesses note the young man's earnest plea ("do not thou yield"). He not only reports but urges the captain to refuse. The verse displays the full disclosure and the timely warning.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the nephew warned the captain not to yield — more than forty men lay in ambush, bound by oath, ready and awaiting only his consent — an earnest plea joined to the full disclosure. The warning came just in time, the conspirators ready to strike. The verse displays the complete exposure of the plot and the young man's urgent plea. The verse marks the timely warning: the captain told the full extent and imminence of the danger. The verse displays the readiness of the conspiracy: forty sworn men, in ambush, awaiting only the captain's word. The verse displays the providence in the timing: the plot exposed at the last moment, before the captain could be deceived into delivering Paul. The young man's earnest plea — "do not thou yield" — displays his concern and the urgency of the danger. The verse displays the full picture of the peril: the ambush, the oath, the readiness, all awaiting the captain's consent. The timely warning, the precise detail, and the urgent plea display the providence that preserved Paul at the critical moment. The verse marks the completion of the disclosure: the captain now fully informed, able to act decisively (vv.23-24). The nephew's warning, exposing the imminent plot, displays God's providence delivering Paul just before the trap could spring — the conspirators ready, but their scheme undone by the timely report.
22:
"So the chief captain then let the young man depart, and charged him, See thou tell no man that thou hast shewed these things to me." The brief verse notes the captain's prudent secrecy. The grammatical voices note his wisdom. Ellicott and Robertson note the captain charged the young man to secrecy, that the conspirators not learn their plot was discovered.

Chrysostom marks the prudence: "it is well done of the tribune also, that he bids him keep it secret, that it might not become known." The captain handled the matter discreetly, planning to act before the conspirators could adapt.

The witnesses note the captain's competence. By securing secrecy, he could foil the plot without alerting the assassins, ensuring Paul's safe removal. The verse displays the captain's prudent handling.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the captain dismissed the young man, charging him to secrecy, that the conspirators not learn their plot was discovered (Ellicott, Robertson) — a prudent handling (Chrysostom: well done to keep it secret), planning to act before they could adapt. The verse displays the captain's wise discretion: securing secrecy to foil the plot. The verse marks the captain's competent handling: discreet, decisive, planning Paul's safe removal. The verse displays the prudence of the officer: by keeping the discovery secret, he could thwart the conspirators without alerting them. The verse displays the providence working through the captain's wisdom: his discretion ensuring the plot's failure and Paul's safe escape. The charge to secrecy, the careful handling, display a capable officer acting decisively. The verse displays the unfolding of the deliverance: the warning received, secrecy secured, the captain about to act (vv.23-24). The captain's prudent discretion, foiling the plot quietly, marks the providence that preserved Paul — the conspiracy undone not by open confrontation but by secret, swift counteraction. The verse displays the wisdom of the officer and the providence that delivered Paul: the plot exposed and quietly thwarted, the apostle to be removed safely to Caesarea before the assassins could strike. The captain's secrecy completes the providential foiling of the conspiracy. The verse displays the quiet competence that often serves God's ends better than open confrontation. The captain neither raised an alarm nor tipped his hand, but simply bound the youth to silence and prepared to act. By this discretion the conspirators were left in ignorance, their ambush rendered useless before they ever knew it had been discovered — providence working through prudence.
23:
"And he called unto him two centurions, saying, Make ready two hundred soldiers to go to Caesarea, and horsemen threescore and ten, and spearmen two hundred, at the third hour of the night;" The witnesses note the large military escort. The grammatical voices note the size: two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, two hundred spearmen — nearly five hundred men to guard one prisoner. The captain took the threat seriously.

On the night march, the witnesses note the secrecy and speed. Setting out at the third hour of the night (9 PM), the escort would be well away before the conspirators learned of it.

The witnesses note the irony and providence: the Roman power deploying a small army to protect Paul from his own countrymen. Chrysostom: "Like some king whom his body-guards escort, so did these convey Paul." The verse displays the great escort.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the captain ordered a large escort — two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, two hundred spearmen, nearly five hundred men — to take Paul to Caesarea by night (the third hour, secretly and swiftly), taking the threat of forty assassins seriously. Chrysostom notes the irony: Paul escorted like a king by his bodyguards. The verse displays the great military escort: the Roman power mobilized to protect one prisoner. The verse marks the captain's decisive action: a small army to guard Paul against the conspirators. The verse displays the providence in the lavish protection: God using the Roman power to deliver Paul from his countrymen's plot. The verse displays the seriousness of the threat: forty sworn assassins requiring nearly five hundred soldiers to ensure Paul's safety. The night march, the large escort, the swift departure display the captain's thorough handling and the providence preserving Paul. The verse displays the irony of the situation: the apostle, hated by his own people, protected by a pagan army. Chrysostom's image — Paul escorted like a king — displays the dignity God conferred on his servant even in custody. The verse marks the providential deliverance: the great escort carrying Paul beyond the reach of the conspirators, fulfilling the Lord's promise that he must bear witness at Rome (v.11). The lavish protection displays God's care for the apostle through the Roman power.
24:
"And provide them beasts, that they may set Paul on, and bring him safe unto Felix the governor." The witnesses note the provision for Paul's safe transport to Felix. The grammatical voices note the "beasts" (mounts) for Paul and perhaps relays. The captain ensured Paul's safe conveyance to the governor at Caesarea.

On Felix, the witnesses note he was the Roman procurator of Judea. Paul was being sent to the proper authority for trial, beyond the reach of the Jerusalem plot.

The witnesses note the captain's thoroughness: providing mounts, ensuring Paul's safe arrival. The verse displays the careful provision for the transfer.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the captain provided mounts for Paul and ordered him brought safely to Felix the governor at Caesarea — careful provision for the transfer to the proper authority, beyond the reach of the plot. The verse displays the thorough arrangement: Paul mounted and safely conveyed to Felix. The verse marks the transfer to the governor: Paul sent to Caesarea for trial. The verse displays the captain's care for Paul's safety: the mounts, the escort, the safe conveyance. The verse displays the providence in the transfer: Paul removed from Jerusalem to Caesarea, beyond the conspirators' reach, toward the appeals that would carry him to Rome. The verse displays the orderly handling: the case referred to Felix, the proper authority. The careful provision — mounts, escort, safe conveyance — displays the captain's thoroughness and the providence preserving Paul. The verse marks the next stage of Paul's journey: from Jerusalem to Caesarea, where he would be tried before Felix (ch. 24), Festus (ch. 25), and Agrippa (ch. 26), and at last sent to Rome. The transfer to Felix, safely arranged, displays God's providence carrying Paul forward — through the Roman legal process, beyond the murderous plot, toward the witness at Rome he was promised (v.11). The verse displays the deliverance accomplished: Paul safely conveyed to the governor, the conspiracy foiled. The verse displays the thoroughness of the deliverance: not merely rescued from the immediate ambush, Paul is mounted, escorted, and conveyed to the proper authority, every detail arranged for his safety. The transfer to Felix moves the apostle out of the murderous reach of Jerusalem and onto the Roman legal road that would lead, through Caesarea, all the way to Rome.
25:
"And he wrote a letter after this manner:" The brief verse introduces Lysias's letter to Felix. The grammatical voices note the official communication. The captain wrote a letter (an elogium, the official report accompanying a prisoner) to explain Paul's case to the governor.

The puritan Henry and others note the providence in the letter: it would serve as a testimony to Paul's innocence, the Roman officer's account vindicating him. The verse introduces the letter (vv.26-30).

The witnesses note the form: an official letter accompanying the prisoner, stating the charges and the captain's findings. The verse sets up the content of Lysias's report.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse, which introduces the letter. The witnesses agree: the captain wrote an official letter (an elogium accompanying the prisoner) to Felix, explaining Paul's case — a communication that would serve, providentially, as a testimony to Paul's innocence (Henry). The verse introduces Lysias's letter (vv.26-30). The verse marks the official report: the captain's account of Paul's case sent to the governor. The verse displays the providence in the letter: the Roman officer's account vindicating Paul, declaring him guilty of no crime worthy of death (v.29). The verse, brief as it is, sets up the content of the letter, which would clear Paul of the serious charges and present his case fairly (if self-servingly) to Felix. The official letter, required to accompany a transferred prisoner, displays the orderly Roman process and the providence using it to testify to Paul's innocence. The verse displays the function of the report: stating the charges, the captain's findings, and his reasons for the transfer. The letter, introduced here, would prove a witness in Paul's favor — the Roman officer's testimony that he had done nothing worthy of death or bonds (v.29). The verse marks the writing of the report that would carry Paul's case to Felix and contribute to the lenient treatment he received. God's providence, working through the Roman legal forms, here produces a written testimony to the apostle's innocence. The verse displays how God turns even the routine machinery of empire to his servant's good. A required document, an officer's self-justifying report, becomes in his hand a written witness to Paul's innocence. The letter that follows would say more than its writer intended — that the apostle had done nothing worthy of death — and so the forms of Rome served the purposes of heaven.
26:
"Claudius Lysias unto the most excellent governor Felix sendeth greeting." The witnesses note the formal opening of the letter, naming the captain. On Claudius Lysias, the grammatical voices note this is the first mention of his name. He had purchased Roman citizenship (22:28), taking the name Claudius (from the emperor Claudius), Lysias being his Greek name.

On "most excellent Felix," the witnesses note the formal, respectful address to the governor. Felix was the procurator of Judea, a freedman who had risen to high office. The greeting follows official form.

The witnesses note the standard epistolary opening (as the Jerusalem Council's letter, 15:23). The verse introduces the sender and recipient of the official report.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the letter opens with the formal greeting — Claudius Lysias (the captain, who had purchased citizenship and taken the name Claudius, 22:28) to the most excellent Felix (the procurator of Judea) — following official epistolary form (as 15:23). The verse displays the formal opening: the named captain addressing the governor respectfully. The verse marks the introduction of the letter's sender and recipient. The verse displays the official character of the communication: the proper form, the respectful address, the named officer. The verse displays the first naming of Claudius Lysias, the captain who had figured in the preceding events. The formal greeting to "most excellent Felix" displays the Roman courtesy and the official channel through which Paul's case proceeded. The verse, brief as it is, sets the official tone of the report. The verse displays the orderly Roman process: the named captain, the titled governor, the formal greeting. The letter's opening marks the proper handling of Paul's transfer through the Roman legal system. The verse displays the providence working through these forms: the official report, addressed to the governor, that would testify to Paul's innocence. The formal greeting introduces the letter that would carry Paul's case to Felix and contribute to his fair treatment, the Roman officer's account vindicating the apostle before the governor. The verse displays the meeting of two worlds in a single line: the freedman-officer who had bought his citizenship and the freedman-governor who ruled a province, both bearing imperial names, both unwittingly serving the advance of a gospel they did not understand. The formal courtesy of the greeting frames the providential report that would carry Paul's cause before the governor of Judea.
27:
"This man was taken of the Jews, and should have been killed of them: then came I with an army, and rescued him, having understood that he was a Roman." The witnesses note Lysias's self-serving account — and the reversal of the facts. On the misrepresentation, the grammatical voices note the colored statement. Ellicott: "The chief captain ingeniously colours his statement so as to claim credit for having rescued a Roman citizen, though, as a matter of fact, he did not discover that he was a citizen until he was on the point of scourging him without a trial. That fact, of course, is passed over without a word."

On the reversed order, Robertson notes: "Lysias did rescue Paul and he did learn that he was a Roman, but in this order. He did not first learn that he was a Roman and then rescue him as his letter states... Lysias simply reversed the order of the facts and omitted the order for scourging Paul to put himself in proper light with Felix."

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence even in the self-serving letter: the captain, to gain favor for Paul, presents him as a wronged Roman — God governing the infidel's tongue for his servant's good. The verse displays the colored but providentially favorable report.

The witnesses agree: Lysias's account was self-serving and reversed the facts — he claims to have rescued Paul "having understood that he was a Roman," though he in fact learned of Paul's citizenship only after, when about to scourge him (Ellicott, Robertson), omitting that illegal order to put himself in a good light with Felix. Robertson notes this reversal, though not quite a lie, twists the order to flatter the captain. Calvin notes the providence even so: the captain, to gain favor for Paul, presents him as a wronged Roman, God governing the infidel's tongue for his servant's good. The verse displays the colored but providentially favorable report. The verse marks the captain's self-serving account: claiming credit, concealing his near-illegal act. The verse displays the human vanity in the report (Lysias polishing his record) and the providence overruling it (the letter favoring Paul). The reversal of the facts — rescuing Paul because he was a Roman, when in truth he learned it after — displays the captain's concern for his own standing. Yet God used even this self-serving letter to commend Paul to Felix as a wronged Roman citizen. The verse displays the providence working through human weakness: the captain's vanity producing a report that vindicated Paul, the infidel's pen serving God's servant.
28:
"And when I would have known the cause wherefore they accused him, I brought him forth into their council:" The brief verse continues Lysias's account of the inquiry before the council. The grammatical voices note the captain's effort to learn the charge. Robertson: "to know fully" the cause. He brought Paul before the Sanhedrin to discover the accusation (22:30).

The witnesses note the captain's reasonable procedure: seeking to understand the charge, he convened the council. This was the inquiry of 22:30-23:10, which ended in the violent division.

The verse displays the captain's account of his attempt to ascertain the cause, leading to the council session. The verse continues the official report.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Lysias reports that, seeking to learn the charge against Paul (Robertson: to know fully), he brought him before the Sanhedrin — the inquiry of 22:30-23:10, which ended in violent division. The verse displays the captain's reasonable procedure: convening the council to discover the accusation. The verse marks the account of the inquiry: the captain's effort to ascertain the cause. The verse displays the orderly Roman process: the captain seeking the charge, bringing Paul before the council. The verse, brief as it is, continues the official report, recounting the captain's attempt to understand the case. The verse displays the captain's diligence: not condemning Paul without knowing the charge, but convening the council to learn it. The account of the inquiry, leading to the council session and its violent outcome (vv.7-10), displays the captain's reasonable handling and the chaos of the Jewish proceeding. The verse displays the contrast between Roman order (the captain seeking the charge) and Jewish disorder (the council's strife). The report continues, the captain explaining his procedure to Felix. The verse marks the recounting of the council inquiry, the next element of the letter that would inform the governor of the case and lead toward the verdict that Paul had done nothing worthy of death (v.29). The captain's account, recounting his diligent inquiry, displays the orderly process that providentially vindicated Paul. The verse displays the contrast that runs through the whole narrative: Roman order seeking the charge before condemning, set against Jewish fury condemning before any charge was proved. The captain's simple effort to learn the cause — convening the council, listening for the accusation — stands as a quiet rebuke to a proceeding that had begun with a blow and ended in a brawl.
29:
"Whom I perceived to be accused of questions of their law, but to have nothing laid to his charge worthy of death or of bonds." The witnesses note Lysias's verdict of Paul's innocence. On "questions of their law," the grammatical voices note the captain's perception. Robertson: "The very distinction drawn by Gallio in Corinth (18:14f.)." The captain saw the matter was a Jewish religious dispute, not a crime against Rome.

On "nothing worthy of death or of bonds," the witnesses note the acquittal. Robertson: "Lysias thus expresses the opinion that Paul ought to be set free." Every Roman magistrate before whom Paul appeared declared him innocent (Gallio, Lysias, Felix, Festus, Agrippa).

The Reformed Calvin notes the limit of the captain's view: a profane man, he counted religious questions trivial; yet his verdict refuted the Jews' charges. The verse displays the Roman acquittal of Paul.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Lysias perceived Paul was accused of Jewish religious questions, not a crime worthy of death or bonds — the distinction Gallio drew (18:14, Robertson) — declaring him innocent of any Roman crime; every Roman magistrate so judged Paul (Gallio, Lysias, Felix, Festus, Agrippa, Robertson). Calvin notes the captain, a profane man, counted religious questions trivial, yet his verdict refuted the Jews' charges. The verse displays the Roman acquittal: Paul guilty of no crime worthy of death. The verse marks the official verdict of innocence: the captain's testimony in Paul's favor. The verse displays the providence in the report: the Roman officer declaring Paul innocent, the charge a mere religious dispute. The verse displays the consistent witness of the Roman authorities: every magistrate found Paul guilty of no crime. The captain's verdict, that Paul deserved neither death nor bonds, displays the injustice of the Jewish persecution and the providence using the Roman power to vindicate the apostle. The verse displays the distinction between Roman law (which found no crime) and Jewish religious zeal (which sought Paul's death over doctrinal questions). The acquittal, recorded in the official letter, contributed to the lenient treatment Paul received and stands as a testimony to his innocence. The verse displays God's providence: the Roman officer's verdict clearing Paul, the persecutor's charge refuted by the very authority they sought to use against him.
30:
"And when it was told me how that the Jews laid wait for the man, I sent straightway to thee, and gave commandment to his accusers also to say before thee what they had against him. Farewell." The witnesses note the close of the letter. The grammatical voices note the captain's account of the plot and his transfer of the case. He reports the ambush (the reason for the transfer) and directs the accusers to bring their charges before Felix.

On directing the accusers to Felix, the Reformed Calvin notes the captain "doth bring the adversaries into contempt, because they went about to kill Paul treacherously" — exposing their malice. The transfer to Felix put the case before proper authority.

On "Farewell," the witnesses note the formal close (as the Jerusalem letter, 15:29, Ellicott). The verse completes the official report. The conspirators' fate is unknown (Ellicott: they likely considered themselves absolved when Paul was gone).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Lysias closes the letter, reporting the plot (the reason for the transfer) and directing the accusers to bring their charges before Felix — exposing their treacherous malice (Calvin) and putting the case before proper authority. The formal "farewell" closes the letter (as 15:29, Ellicott). The verse displays the completion of the report: the plot disclosed, the case transferred, the accusers summoned. The verse marks the close of Lysias's letter: the official account of Paul's case sent to Felix. The verse displays the providence in the transfer: the case moved to Caesarea, the accusers required to appear before the governor. The verse displays the captain's exposure of the Jews' malice: their treacherous plot revealed, casting doubt on their charges (Calvin: if their cause were just, they would have trusted the law, not sought murder). The verse displays the orderly transfer: the case before Felix, the accusers summoned, the proper process engaged. The conspirators' fate is unknown — likely they considered themselves absolved when Paul was gone (Ellicott). The verse marks the end of the letter and the providential transfer of Paul's case to Caesarea, beyond the reach of the plot, toward the appeals that would carry him to Rome. The official report, exposing the malice and transferring the case, displays God's providence using the Roman process to preserve and vindicate Paul.
31:
"Then the soldiers, as it was commanded them, took Paul, and brought him by night to Antipatris." The witnesses note the night march to Antipatris. The grammatical voices note the distance and speed. Robertson: "Through the night, travelling by night forty miles from Jerusalem to Antipatris... a hard night's ride." The escort moved swiftly under cover of darkness.

Chrysostom marks the royal escort: "Like some king whom his body-guards escort, so did these convey Paul; in such numbers too, and by night, for fear of the wrath of the people." The large escort, the night march, displayed both the danger and the providence.

On Antipatris, the witnesses note it was built by Herod, about thirty-five to forty miles from Jerusalem, beyond the danger zone. The verse displays the swift, safe removal of Paul.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the soldiers took Paul by night to Antipatris (about forty miles, a hard night's ride, Robertson) — a swift march under cover of darkness, the large escort conveying him like a king (Chrysostom), for fear of the people's wrath. Antipatris (built by Herod) was beyond the danger zone. The verse displays the swift, safe removal of Paul from Jerusalem. The verse marks the night march: the escort carrying Paul beyond the conspirators' reach. The verse displays the providence in the swift removal: Paul carried safely away before the plot could strike. The verse displays the magnitude of the protection: a forty-mile night march with nearly five hundred soldiers to preserve one prisoner. The royal escort, the night journey, the safe arrival at Antipatris display the providence delivering Paul. Chrysostom's image — Paul conveyed like a king — displays the dignity God conferred on his servant. The verse displays the foiling of the plot: Paul removed under cover of darkness, the conspirators left behind. The verse marks the providential deliverance: the apostle carried beyond the reach of the forty assassins, toward Caesarea and the witness at Rome he was promised (v.11). The swift, safe march to Antipatris displays God's care for Paul through the Roman power, the conspiracy thwarted, the apostle preserved.
32:
"On the morrow they left the horsemen to go with him, and returned to the castle:" The brief verse notes the foot soldiers returning. The grammatical voices note the arrangement: at Antipatris, beyond the danger, the foot soldiers (the four hundred) returned to Jerusalem, leaving the seventy horsemen to escort Paul the rest of the way to Caesarea.

On the reason, the witnesses note the safety. Barnes and Clarke: "as they were then beyond the danger of the conspirators, the soldiers who had guarded them thus far returned." The horsemen sufficed for the remaining journey.

The Reformed Calvin notes the captain's prudence: not sending the whole garrison far away, but only as far as needed for safety. The verse displays the orderly handling of the escort.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: at Antipatris, beyond the danger, the foot soldiers returned to Jerusalem, leaving the seventy horsemen to escort Paul to Caesarea (Barnes, Clarke) — the horsemen sufficing for the safer remaining journey, the captain prudent not to send the whole garrison far (Calvin). The verse displays the orderly handling of the escort: the foot soldiers returning, the horsemen continuing. The verse marks the second stage of the journey: Paul, now safe from the plot, escorted by the cavalry alone. The verse displays the captain's prudence: deploying the large force only as far as the danger required, then returning the foot soldiers. The verse displays the providence in the safe passage: Paul beyond the conspirators' reach, the lighter escort sufficient. The verse, brief as it is, displays the careful management of the transfer: the foot soldiers returning, the horsemen completing the journey. The verse displays the diminishing danger: at Antipatris the threat was past, and the cavalry could safely convey Paul the rest of the way. The orderly arrangement — foot soldiers back, horsemen on — displays the captain's competence and the providence preserving Paul. The verse marks the continuation of the safe journey to Caesarea, the apostle now beyond the plot, escorted by the horsemen toward Felix and the next stage of his witness. The verse displays the deliverance accomplished: Paul safely past the danger, the conspiracy left behind.
33:
"Who, when they came to Caesarea, and delivered the epistle to the governor, presented Paul also before him." The witnesses note the arrival at Caesarea and the presentation to Felix. The grammatical voices note the delivery: the horsemen delivered Lysias's letter and presented Paul to the governor. Robertson notes the fulfillment of Agabus's prophecy (21:11) — Paul delivered to the Gentiles, now come true.

On the presentation, the witnesses note the orderly process: the prisoner and the report handed over to Felix, the proper authority. The case was now before the governor.

The witnesses note the providence: Paul safely arrived at Caesarea, the plot foiled, the prophecy fulfilled. The verse displays the safe arrival and presentation.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the horsemen reached Caesarea, delivered Lysias's letter, and presented Paul to Felix — the orderly transfer to the proper authority, fulfilling Agabus's prophecy (21:11, Robertson: Paul delivered to the Gentiles). The verse displays the safe arrival and presentation: Paul before the governor, the case transferred. The verse marks the completion of the transfer: Paul safely at Caesarea, the plot foiled. The verse displays the providence in the safe arrival: the apostle preserved through the journey, presented to Felix. The verse displays the fulfillment of the prophecy: Agabus had foretold Paul's binding and delivery to the Gentiles (21:11), now accomplished. The verse displays the orderly Roman process: the letter delivered, the prisoner presented, the case before the governor. The safe arrival at Caesarea, beyond the conspirators' reach, displays God's providence preserving Paul. The verse marks the next stage: Paul before Felix, awaiting the hearing (ch. 24). The presentation to the governor, with the letter testifying to his innocence (v.29), displays the providence carrying Paul through the Roman legal system toward the witness at Rome. The verse displays the deliverance accomplished and the prophecy fulfilled: the apostle safely conveyed to Caesarea, the plot defeated, the case before the proper authority, the journey toward Rome continuing under God's providence. The verse displays the convergence of prophecy and providence: Agabus had bound his own hands to foretell this hour, and now Paul stands, a prisoner, before the Gentile governor exactly as foretold.
