The safe arrival at Caesarea, the letter delivered, the apostle presented — every detail confirms that the word of the Spirit through the prophet had not fallen to the ground.
34:
"And when the governor had read the letter, he asked of what province he was. And when he understood that he was of Cilicia;" The witnesses note Felix's inquiry into Paul's province. The grammatical voices note the question's purpose. Barnes: "to ascertain whether he properly belonged to his jurisdiction." Felix, learning Paul was a Roman from the letter, asked his province to determine jurisdiction.

On Cilicia, the witnesses note it was Paul's native province (Tarsus, 21:39). Ellicott notes the natural question (as Pilate asked of Jesus, Luke 23:6), though Felix chose to take cognizance of the case despite Paul belonging to another province.

The witnesses note the orderly legal process: Felix establishing jurisdiction before proceeding. The verse displays the governor's proper inquiry.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Felix, having read the letter, asked Paul's province to determine jurisdiction (Barnes), learning he was of Cilicia (Paul's native province, Tarsus, 21:39) — a natural question (as Pilate's of Jesus, Luke 23:6, Ellicott), though Felix chose to take the case despite Paul belonging to another province. The verse displays the governor's proper inquiry: establishing jurisdiction before proceeding. The verse marks Felix's orderly handling: the question of province, the decision to hear the case. The verse displays the Roman legal process: jurisdiction ascertained, the case taken up. The verse displays the providence in Felix's decision: choosing to hear Paul's case, advancing the legal process that would carry the apostle toward Rome. The verse displays the orderly procedure: Felix reading the letter, asking the province, deciding to proceed. The inquiry, establishing that Paul was of Cilicia, displays the governor's attention to jurisdiction. The verse marks the engagement of the Roman legal machinery in Paul's case: Felix taking cognizance, setting the hearing (v.35). The verse displays God's providence working through the proper legal channels: the governor's inquiry, his decision to hear, advancing Paul's case toward the appeals that would bring him to Rome. The verse displays the orderly process by which Paul, preserved from the plot, would now be tried before Felix (ch. 24), the next stage of his journey under divine providence.
35:
"I will hear thee, said he, when thine accusers are also come. And he commanded him to be kept in Herod's judgment hall." The witnesses note Felix's deferral of the hearing and Paul's custody. The grammatical voices note the proper procedure: Felix would hear Paul when his accusers arrived, ensuring both sides present (as Roman law required, the principle Lysias had invoked).

On "Herod's judgment hall" (praetorium), the witnesses note Paul was kept in the palace built by Herod the Great, now the governor's residence at Caesarea. Chrysostom: "again Paul is put in bonds." Paul was held in custody, awaiting the hearing.

The witnesses note the providence: Paul safely in Roman custody, the hearing deferred until the accusers came, the legal process engaged. The verse closes the chapter with Paul awaiting trial at Caesarea.

The thin comment matches the verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Felix deferred the hearing until Paul's accusers arrived (ensuring both sides present, as Roman law required), and kept Paul in Herod's praetorium (the palace at Caesarea, now the governor's residence) — Paul again in custody (Chrysostom), awaiting trial. The verse displays the proper procedure and Paul's custody: the hearing deferred, the apostle held safely. The verse marks the close of the chapter: Paul at Caesarea, awaiting the hearing before Felix (ch. 24). The verse displays the providence in the safe custody: Paul preserved from the plot, held in the Roman palace, the legal process engaged. The verse displays the orderly Roman procedure: the accusers required to appear, both sides to be heard. The custody in Herod's praetorium displays the dignity of Paul's confinement — held in the governor's palace, not a common prison. The verse displays the next stage of Paul's journey: from the Sanhedrin's plot to Roman custody at Caesarea, awaiting the trials that would carry him to Rome. The chapter that began with Paul before the hostile Sanhedrin closes with him safely in Roman custody, delivered from the conspiracy, the legal process advancing under God's providence. The verse displays the fulfillment of the Lord's promise (v.11): Paul preserved, the witness at Rome drawing nearer through the orderly Roman process. The deferred hearing and safe custody mark the providential preservation of the apostle for the testimony ahead.
